source_intake_record.raw_question_text: A heavy symmetrical top with one point fixed is precessing at a steady angular velocity $\Omega$ about the vertical axis $z$. What is the minimum spin $\omega^{\prime}$ about its symmetrical axis $z^{\prime}$ (where $z^{\prime}$ is inclined at an angle $\theta$ with respect to the $z$-axis)? The top has mass $m$ and its center of gravity is at a distance $h$ from the fixed point. Use the coordinate systems indicated in Fig. 1.193, with the axes $z, z^{\prime}, x$ and $x^{\prime}$ in the same plane at the time under consideration and assume $I_{1}=I_{2}$.
extraction_notes: images field contains a PIL image object representation rather than a file path or URL

candidate_problem_record.subject: 物理
candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: A heavy symmetrical top with one point fixed is precessing at a steady angular velocity $\Omega$ about the vertical axis $z$. What is the minimum spin $\omega^{\prime}$ about its symmetrical axis $z^{\prime}$ (where $z^{\prime}$ is inclined at an angle $\theta$ with respect to the $z$-axis)? The top has mass $m$ and its center of gravity is at a distance $h$ from the fixed point. Use the coordinate systems indicated in Fig. 1.193, with the axes $z, z^{\prime}, x$ and $x^{\prime}$ in the same plane at the time under consideration and assume $I_{1}=I_{2}$.
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: images field contains a PIL image object representation rather than a file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain: Referring to the Eulerian angles defined in Problem 1212, the torque due to gravity is in a direction perpendicular to the $xz$-plane and in the rotating frame $Ox^{\prime}y^{\prime}z^{\prime}$ attached to the top has components $mgh\sin\theta\sin\psi$, $mgh\sin\theta\cos\psi$, $0$. Euler's equations, which apply in the rotating frame, are, for $I_{1}=I_{2}$, $$ I_{1}\dot{\omega}_{x^{\prime}}-(I_{1}-I_{3})\omega_{y^{\prime}}\omega_{z^{\prime}} = mgh\sin\theta\sin\psi, \quad I_{1}\dot{\omega}_{y^{\prime}}-(I_{3}-I_{1})\omega_{x^{\prime}}\omega_{z^{\prime}} = mgh\sin\theta\cos\psi, \quad I_{3}\dot{\omega}_{z^{\prime}} = 0. $$ The angular velocity vector $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ in the rotating frame has components $$ -\dot{\varphi}\sin\theta\cos\psi, \quad \dot{\varphi}\sin\theta\sin\psi, \quad \dot{\varphi}\cos\theta+\dot{\psi} $$ as $\dot{\theta}=0$. So writing $\Omega$ for $\dot{\varphi}$ and noting that $\ddot{\varphi}=0$ for steady precession, the first Euler's equation becomes $$ \Omega^{2}(I_{1}-I_{3})\cos\theta-\Omega I_{3}\dot{\psi}+mgh=0, $$ giving $$ \omega^{\prime}\equiv\dot{\psi}=\frac{mgh+(I_{1}-I_{3})\Omega^{2}\cos\theta}{I_{3}\Omega}. $$ However for $\Omega$ to be real we require that $$ I_{3}^{2}\omega^{\prime 2}-4(I_{1}-I_{3})mgh\cos\theta \ge 0, $$ or $$ \omega^{\prime} \ge \frac{1}{I_{3}}\sqrt{4(I_{1}-I_{3})mgh\cos\theta}. $$
